label: task_profile_kotlin_android_studio_iterative.json
description: Tasks are auto-generated from Android Kotlin focus areas (architecture, DB, Compose, resources/constants).

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Dependency Stability and Version Policy
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Dependency Stability and Version Policy

Area guidance:
Check: (1) all dependencies in libs.versions.toml use stable releases only — no alpha/beta/rc/snapshot versions unless there is a documented stable-fallback strategy, (2) AGP, Kotlin, Compose BOM, and KSP versions are mutually compatible and documented, (3) unused or duplicate dependencies are removed, (4) version catalog (libs.versions.toml) is used consistently — no hardcoded version strings in build.gradle.kts, (5) dependency tree has no conflicting transitive versions that override each other silently.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew dependencies (dependency tree)
- ./gradlew :app:dependencies --configuration releaseRuntimeClasspath
- ./gradlew build (detects version conflicts at compile time)
- Manual review of gradle/libs.versions.toml

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: apply either dependency file changes (libs.versions.toml, build.gradle.kts) or documentation for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.
- Respect production-grade Android Kotlin standards (Compose, Room, resources, constants).
- Evaluate whether required/optional checks are sufficient for this area and list missing or upgrade candidates.
- Organize findings into the standard output sections as follows:
  ## Summary — Focus Area Goal, Priority, key findings overview
  ## Plan — Audit Criteria, Concrete Refactorings / Improvements, Small Kotlin Example
  ## Acceptance Checklist — Best Practices as pass/fail items, Common Mistakes as anti-pattern checks
  ## Risks — Dependencies To Other Areas, Irritations / Failure Injection and Expected Behavior

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Architecture and Layer Boundaries
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Architecture and Layer Boundaries

Area guidance:
Check: (1) strict UI/Domain/Data layer separation — UI layer contains only Composables and ViewModels, no repository or database references, (2) UDF (Unidirectional Data Flow) is enforced — UI emits events upward, ViewModel exposes state downward, (3) ViewModel does not hold direct Activity/Fragment/Context references (use Application context only when necessary), (4) Repository interface is in domain layer, implementation in data layer — UI never imports data-layer types directly, (5) module or package structure reflects layer boundaries with no cross-layer imports in the wrong direction.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew lint (architecture-related lint rules)
- ./gradlew build (detects missing DI bindings)
- Manual import-path audit per layer package

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.
- Respect production-grade Android Kotlin standards (Compose, Room, resources, constants).
- Evaluate whether required/optional checks are sufficient for this area and list missing or upgrade candidates.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Dependency Injection, KSP, and Coroutine Scope Contracts
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Dependency Injection, KSP, and Coroutine Scope Contracts

Area guidance:
Check: (1) Hilt modules use @Binds for interface-to-impl binding and @Provides only when construction cannot be annotated directly, (2) KSP is used exclusively — no kapt processors present, (3) application-level CoroutineScope is created with SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Default and injected via Hilt rather than accessed as a global singleton, (4) component scopes are appropriate — @ViewModelScoped for VM-tied dependencies, @Singleton only for truly app-wide state, (5) qualifier annotations (@Named or custom) are used when multiple bindings of the same type exist.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew build (KSP annotation processing errors surface here)
- ./gradlew :app:kspDebugKotlin (run KSP separately)
- ./gradlew lint

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Kotlin Coroutines and Flow Quality
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Kotlin Coroutines and Flow Quality

Area guidance:
Check: (1) structured concurrency is respected — all coroutines launched within a defined scope (viewModelScope, applicationScope), no GlobalScope usage, (2) Flow operators are used correctly: catch{} before terminal operators, onEach{} not misused as a side-effect replacement for collect{}, conflate()/collectLatest{} used for UI-rate-limited streams, (3) StateFlow is used for UI state (single current value), SharedFlow for events (fire-and-forget); cold Flow is not shared to multiple collectors without stateIn/shareIn, (4) stateIn() uses WhileSubscribed(stopTimeoutMillis = 5000) to survive config changes while releasing resources in background, (5) exceptions in coroutines are not silently swallowed — use CoroutineExceptionHandler or structured propagation.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew test (Flow unit tests; add app:turbine as testImplementation for Flow testing)
- ./gradlew lint
- Note: add 'app.cash.turbine:turbine' as testImplementation for Flow assertion testing

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:

  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Null Safety and Kotlin Type System
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Null Safety and Kotlin Type System

Area guidance:
Check: (1) no !! (non-null assertion) operator in production code — replace with safe calls ?., let{}, ?: elvis, or require()/checkNotNull(), (2) sealed class and when expressions are exhaustive — all branches handled, no else-only fallbacks hiding missed cases, (3) data class contracts are correct: equals()/hashCode()/copy() behave as expected; mutable properties in data classes are justified and documented, (4) Java interop nullability: all @Nullable/@NonNull annotations from Java APIs are treated as potentially null in Kotlin, (5) lateinit var is audited — isInitialized checked where needed; prefer lazy{} for read-only late initialization.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew lint (NullAway if configured, Kotlin null-safety lint checks)
- ./gradlew build (exhaustiveness warnings as errors when -Werror is enabled)
- Manual audit of !! operator occurrences

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.
- Respect production-grade Android Kotlin standards (Compose, Room, resources, constants).

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: State Management and Compose Lifecycle
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
State Management and Compose Lifecycle

Area guidance:
Check: (1) state hoisting is applied — stateful composables delegate state ownership to ViewModel or parent, (2) UiState is immutable (data class or sealed class with val fields only) — no mutable state exposed from ViewModel as var or MutableStateFlow directly, (3) collectAsStateWithLifecycle() is used instead of collectAsState() for lifecycle-aware collection (stops collection in background), (4) side effects use the correct Compose effect API: LaunchedEffect for suspending work, SideEffect for non-Compose system calls, DisposableEffect for cleanup on leave, (5) recompositions triggered by state changes are scoped — state reads happen in the lowest-level composable that needs them.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew lint (Compose-specific lint rules via AGP)
- ./gradlew test (ViewModel unit tests)
- Android Studio Layout Inspector (recomposition count visualization)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.
- Respect production-grade Android Kotlin standards (Compose, Room, resources, constants).
- Evaluate whether required/optional checks are sufficient for this area and list missing or upgrade candidates.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Compose Recomposition and Rendering Performance
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Compose Recomposition and Rendering Performance

Area guidance:
Check: (1) high-frequency state (scroll position, animation progress) is read in the draw/layout phase (Modifier.graphicsLayer{}, Modifier.offset{}) rather than the composition phase to avoid recomposition loops, (2) unstable lambda parameters are stabilized with remember { } — particularly event handler lambdas referencing ViewModel, (3) derivedStateOf{} is used when a derived value should only trigger recomposition when the derived result changes (not on every source change), (4) large lists use LazyColumn/LazyRow with stable keys (key = { item.id }) to prevent unnecessary item recompositions, (5) @Stable or @Immutable annotations are applied to data classes used as Compose parameters when the compiler cannot infer stability.

Key tools for this area:
- Android Studio Layout Inspector with recomposition counts enabled
- ./gradlew lint (Compose stability lint rules)
- ./gradlew assembleDebug (check for compose-compiler metrics: add -P plugin:androidx.compose.compiler.plugins.kotlin:reportsDestination=... to kotlinOptions)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Code Quality, Resources, and Localization
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Code Quality, Resources, and Localization

Area guidance:
Check: (1) all user-facing strings are in res/values/strings.xml — no hardcoded string literals in Composables or ViewModels, (2) UiText sealed class (UiText.StringResource / UiText.DynamicString) is used to pass UI strings from ViewModel to UI layer without Android context in ViewModel, (3) Timber is used consistently for all logging (no android.util.Log or println in production code), (4) dimension tokens and spacing constants are centralized (MaterialTheme.spacing or a custom theme extension) — no magic dp/sp literals scattered in Composables, (5) content descriptions are present on all interactive and informational Composables that lack visible text labels (accessibility).

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew lint (HardcodedText, MissingTranslation, ContentDescription lint rules)
- ./gradlew lintDebug -- produces HTML report at app/build/reports/lint-results-debug.html

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Navigation Strategy and Route Safety
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Navigation Strategy and Route Safety

Area guidance:
Check: (1) navigation route constants are defined in a sealed class or object — no raw string literals used as route values in NavHost or navigate() calls, (2) arguments passed via navigation routes are validated on the receiving screen before use — missing or wrong-type args handled gracefully, (3) back-stack behavior is correct: popUpTo + launchSingleTop used on bottom-navigation items to prevent stack accumulation, (4) deep link URIs are declared in the manifest and tested for cold-start and in-app launch scenarios, (5) navigation actions are triggered from ViewModel (via event Flow/channel) or composable event callbacks — not from LaunchedEffect with direct navController references captured in lambdas.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew lint (navigation-related lint rules)
- ./gradlew build (missing route argument compilation errors)
- Manual NavHost audit

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Error Handling and UI State Patterns
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Error Handling and UI State Patterns

Area guidance:
Check: (1) UiState is a sealed class or data class with explicit Loading, Success, and Error subtypes — no nullable fields used as implicit loading/error signals, (2) all exceptions in ViewModel coroutines are caught and mapped to user-facing UiState.Error with a UiText message — no raw exception messages shown to the user, (3) network and DB errors are distinguished from logic errors so retry is offered only when appropriate, (4) empty state is an explicit UiState.Empty variant — not confused with the initial Loading state or a null list, (5) one-time UI events (snackbars, navigation triggers) are delivered via SharedFlow or Channel, not via StateFlow that would re-deliver on recomposition.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew test (ViewModel unit tests with fake repositories)
- ./gradlew lint
- Note: add 'io.mockk:mockk' as testImplementation for ViewModel unit testing with fakes

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Database and Persistence (Room / Repository / Flow)
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Database and Persistence (Room / Repository / Flow)

Area guidance:
Check: (1) Room entities have explicit primary keys, column types, nullability constraints, and indexes on frequently queried columns, (2) all Room write operations use @Transaction where multiple tables are written atomically, (3) DAO query projections return only needed columns (use data class projections or @ColumnInfo subsets) — no SELECT * on large entities, (4) Room schema export is enabled (exportSchema = true) and schema JSON files are committed to version control for migration safety, (5) Flow from DAO is mapped in Repository to domain models before being exposed to ViewModel — ViewModel never imports Room entity types.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew test (Room in-memory database tests with TestCoroutineDispatcher)
- ./gradlew :app:kspDebugKotlin (Room schema validation and migration checks)
- ./gradlew lint
- Schema export: check app/schemas/ directory for committed JSON files

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Network and Data Resilience
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Network and Data Resilience

Area guidance:
Check: (1) OkHttp client has explicit connect, read, and write timeouts configured — no reliance on defaults (unlimited), (2) HTTP caching is configured via Cache(dir, size) for cacheable responses — repeated identical requests do not always hit network, (3) large payloads (lists, media metadata) use streaming or pagination (Paging 3 is present) rather than loading full response into memory, (4) all API responses are wrapped in a Result<T> or sealed NetworkResult type before leaving the data layer — callers never handle HttpException or IOException directly, (5) Retrofit/OkHttp are configured on Dispatchers.IO — no network calls on Dispatchers.Main.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew test (MockWebServer is already in the project — use it for network layer tests)
- ./gradlew lint
- MockWebServer: com.squareup.okhttp3:mockwebserver (already in libs.versions.toml)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Memory Leaks and Object Lifecycle
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Memory Leaks and Object Lifecycle

Area guidance:
Check: (1) ViewModel never holds a reference to Activity, Fragment, View, or NavController — these are lifecycle-shorter than ViewModel and cause leaks, (2) DisposableEffect in Compose registers and properly unregisters listeners, BroadcastReceivers, or callbacks in the onDispose block, (3) Kotlin coroutines launched with viewModelScope or lifecycleScope are automatically cancelled — avoid GlobalScope or manually managed Job references that outlive the component, (4) Coil image requests are scoped to the composable lifecycle and do not hold Bitmap references beyond the composable's lifetime, (5) Media3 / ExoPlayer player instances are released in the appropriate lifecycle callback (onStop or onDestroy) — not only when a new player is created.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew lint (Leak-related lint rules)
- Note: add 'com.squareup.leakcanary:leakcanary-android' as debugImplementation to detect leaks at runtime during development
- Android Studio Memory Profiler (manual heap dump analysis)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Rendering Loops and Data Granularity
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Rendering Loops and Data Granularity

Area guidance:
Check: (1) high-frequency rendering loops (e.g. audio progress at 60fps) isolate fast-changing state into a separate StateFlow — not mixed into the main UiState that triggers full-screen recomposition, (2) Room DAO queries project only the columns needed for the current view (custom data class projections) — full entity is not fetched when only 2-3 fields are displayed, (3) Paging 3 is used for lists longer than ~50 items — no full list loaded into a single StateFlow<List<T>>, (4) write operations that cause Room invalidation are batched where possible to reduce Flow emissions triggering recomposition, (5) Coil image requests use memoryCachePolicy and diskCachePolicy to avoid redundant decode/download cycles in scroll lists.

Key tools for this area:
- Android Studio Layout Inspector (recomposition highlight)
- Android Studio CPU Profiler (frame time analysis)
- ./gradlew lint
- Paging 3: already in project (androidx.paging)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Security, Privacy, and Permissions
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Security, Privacy, and Permissions

Area guidance:
Check: (1) AndroidManifest.xml has no android:exported=true on components (Activity, Service, Receiver, Provider) that are not intended for inter-app access — all exported components validate caller identity, (2) secrets (API keys, tokens) are not in source code, string resources, or BuildConfig — use EncryptedSharedPreferences or a secure backend, (3) permissions are requested only at the point of actual use (just-in-time) and only the minimum set required is declared in the manifest, (4) DataStore or EncryptedSharedPreferences is used for sensitive user data — plain SharedPreferences not used for tokens or session data, (5) network security config (res/xml/network_security_config.xml) is present to prevent cleartext HTTP traffic in release builds.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew lint (ExportedReceiver, HardcodedDebugMode, AllowBackup, NetworkSecurityConfig lint rules)
- ./gradlew lintRelease (stricter than lintDebug for security rules)
- apksigner.bat: C:\Users\jemi\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk\build-tools\36.1.0\apksigner.bat (verify release APK signing)
- Manual AndroidManifest.xml audit

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Process Death and State Restoration
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Process Death and State Restoration

Area guidance:
Check: (1) all ephemeral UI state that must survive process death (selected tab, scroll position, form input) is saved via SavedStateHandle in ViewModel — not only in Compose rememberSaveable, (2) cold start with restored state produces the same visual result as normal navigation — no null pointer crashes or empty screens from missing restored state, (3) configuration changes (rotation, night mode toggle, font scale) do not reset state or trigger unnecessary network refetch, (4) ViewModel's SavedStateHandle types are limited to Parcelable, Bundle-compatible primitives, or Serializable — complex objects are serialized before storage, (5) DataStore is used for user preferences that must persist across process death and app reinstalls.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew test (SavedStateHandle can be instantiated in unit tests)
- adb.exe: C:\Users\jemi\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk\platform-tools\adb.exe
  - adb shell am kill <package> (simulate process death)
  - adb shell am start -n <package>/<activity> (cold start verification)
- Android Studio App Actions tester (deep link cold start)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.
- Respect production-grade Android Kotlin standards (Compose, Room, resources, constants).
- Evaluate whether required/optional checks are sufficient for this area and list missing or upgrade candidates.
- Organize findings into the standard output sections as follows:
  ## Summary — Focus Area Goal, Priority, key findings overview
  ## Plan — Audit Criteria, Concrete Refactorings / Improvements, Small Kotlin Example
  ## Acceptance Checklist — Best Practices as pass/fail items, Common Mistakes as anti-pattern checks
  ## Risks — Dependencies To Other Areas, Irritations / Failure Injection and Expected Behavior

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Background Work Reliability
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Background Work Reliability

Area guidance:
Check: (1) WorkManager Workers define a correct retry and backoff policy (setBackoffCriteria with EXPONENTIAL strategy) — default linear backoff is rarely appropriate for network-dependent work, (2) Workers are idempotent — re-running a Worker after a partial execution produces the correct result without duplicates or data corruption, (3) Constraints are set appropriately (NETWORK_CONNECTED, BATTERY_NOT_LOW) — work is not attempted when it is guaranteed to fail, (4) Worker cancellation is checked via isStopped and long-running work is interrupted promptly on cancellation signal, (5) Hilt-injected Workers use HiltWorker + @AssistedInject — not constructor injection that bypasses WorkManager's factory.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew test (WorkManager TestDriver for controlling task execution in unit tests)
- ./gradlew lint
- adb.exe: C:\Users\jemi\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk\platform-tools\adb.exe
  - adb shell dumpsys jobscheduler (inspect pending/running WorkManager jobs)
- WorkManager: androidx.work:work-runtime-ktx (already in project)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.

  - Implementation: modify code only for this focus area.

- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.
- Respect production-grade Android Kotlin standards (Compose, Room, resources, constants).

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Android Testing Strategy (Unit + Instrumented + UI)
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Android Testing Strategy (Unit + Instrumented + UI)

Area guidance:
Check: (1) test pyramid is balanced — majority unit tests (JVM, fast), fewer instrumented (device), fewest full UI tests (slow); large gaps in ViewModel or Repository unit tests are a high-priority risk, (2) Compose UI tests use onNodeWithText/onNodeWithTag with semantic matchers — not pixel-based or implementation-detail assertions, (3) fake/stub repositories are used in ViewModel unit tests rather than mocking Room DAOs directly, (4) MockWebServer (already in project) is used for network layer tests to verify request/response handling without real network, (5) test coroutines use kotlinx-coroutines-test with UnconfinedTestDispatcher or StandardTestDispatcher and advanceUntilIdle() — no real delays or Thread.sleep in tests.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew test (JVM unit tests)
- ./gradlew connectedDebugAndroidTest (requires connected device or emulator)
- ./gradlew :app:testDebugUnitTest --tests "com.example.*" (run specific test class)
- MockWebServer: com.squareup.okhttp3:mockwebserver (already in project)
- Note: add 'io.mockk:mockk' as testImplementation for clean fake/mock setup in ViewModel tests
- Note: add 'app.cash.turbine:turbine' as testImplementation for Flow assertion in coroutine tests

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: write or extend tests for this focus area.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.
- Respect production-grade Android Kotlin standards (Compose, Room, resources, constants).
- Evaluate whether required/optional checks are sufficient for this area and list missing or upgrade candidates.
- Organize findings into the standard output sections as follows:

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Code Quality Tooling (Detekt / Ktlint)
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Code Quality Tooling (Detekt / Ktlint)

Area guidance:
Check: (1) Detekt Gradle plugin is configured in build.gradle.kts with a committed detekt.yml baseline — complexity, naming, and style rules are enforced as build failures, (2) Ktlint (via Gradle plugin or standalone) enforces consistent formatting — no manual formatting debates in code review, (3) a lint.xml baseline file captures existing lint warnings so new violations are immediately visible without noise from legacy code, (4) all quality checks (lint, detekt, ktlint) run in CI as part of every pull request build, (5) max complexity thresholds are set per function (cyclomatic ≤ 10) and class (line count ≤ 200) in detekt.yml.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew detekt (after adding: id("io.gitlab.arturbosch.detekt") in build.gradle.kts)
- ./gradlew ktlintCheck (after adding: id("org.jlleitschuh.gradle.ktlint") in build.gradle.kts)
- ./gradlew lint (always available via AGP)
- Note: Detekt and Ktlint are NOT currently installed — this iteration adds them as Gradle plugins

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.
  - Implementation: apply either Gradle plugin configuration and baseline generation, or document the setup steps.
- If adjacent issues are found, list them as dependencies but do not widen scope.
- Include at least two irritation/failure scenarios for this area and define expected behavior.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Release Hardening and Build Safety
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Release Hardening and Build Safety

Area guidance:
Check: (1) release build has isMinifyEnabled = true and isShrinkResources = true with a proguard-rules.pro that keeps Retrofit/Room/Hilt/Jackson models and entry points, (2) ProGuard/R8 rules are tested by building and running assembleRelease — the release APK launches without ClassNotFoundException or IllegalAccessException, (3) APK is signed and verified: apksigner verify --verbose app-release.apk confirms valid signature and no debug certificate in release, (4) android:debuggable is not set to true in any release build type (lint catches this), (5) compileSdk and targetSdk are at or near the latest stable API level to comply with Play Store requirements.

Key tools for this area:
- ./gradlew assembleRelease (build release APK with R8)
- apksigner.bat: C:\Users\jemi\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk\build-tools\36.1.0\apksigner.bat
  - apksigner verify --verbose --print-certs app\build\outputs\apk\release\app-release.apk
- zipalign.exe: C:\Users\jemi\AppData\Local\Android\Sdk\build-tools\36.1.0\zipalign.exe
- ./gradlew lintRelease (stricter security rules in release variant)

Execution rules:
- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:

  - Implementation: apply either build configuration changes or documentation for this focus area.

iterations.title: Kotlin Android Studio: Final Synthesis and Prioritized Roadmap
iterations.prompt:
Kotlin Android Studio iterative mode is enabled.

Overall goal:
Improve Kotlin Android code quality for production readiness.

Current focus area:
Final Synthesis and Prioritized Roadmap

Area guidance:
Aggregate findings from all previously completed focus areas into a single prioritized action plan. Group items into four tiers: (1) Critical / Blocking — must fix before any production release, (2) High / Ship-risk — fix before GA, (3) Medium / Technical Debt — next sprint, (4) Low / Nice-to-have — backlog. For each item include: specific file and class/function reference, root cause in one sentence, and estimated effort (S/M/L). If a prior area was skipped or not completed, note it explicitly with the reason.

Key tools for this area:
- (no new tools — summarize findings from previous areas)

- Keep scope limited to this focus area.
- Respect phase boundaries:
  - Evaluation and Review: analyze only, do not modify code.

- Make at least one meaningful documentation change. If docs/final_synthesis_prioritized_roadmap.md already exists and is complete, update another relevant docs/*.md file and explain why.
